I wish I had more time before the launch.
I wish I had asked for help sooner.
If only I had not ignored my gut feeling.
I wish investors would decide faster.
You have come a long way, Eliane. Looking back on the year, is there anything you would do differently?
Honestly, I wish I had asked for help sooner. I tried to learn everything alone, and the learning curve was brutal.
That is a common blind spot for founders. But look at your resilience, every setback, you bounced back.
Maybe. There was one moment I almost cut my losses. If only I had not doubted the pilot, I would have moved faster.
Self-doubt visits everyone. What did you learn from it?
To trust my gut feeling. In hindsight, my instinct was usually right, I just wish I had listened to it.
And the hardest trade-off?
Growth versus control. I wish investors would decide faster, but I understand the scrutiny now.
You have grown. The founder who wishes she had known everything is not the one who wins, it is the one who keeps learning.
I wish the board would decide faster.
In hindsight, every setback was a wake-up call.
I wish I were more confident in the room.

Hi Eliane, it is Henry. I have been thinking about our conversation. Every founder I have backed says the same thing in hindsight: I wish I had trusted myself sooner. When you hit a setback, the temptation is to freeze and to doubt every decision. But the founders who last are the ones with resilience. They bounce back and treat each mistake as a wake-up call, not a reason to quit. Do not fall into the blind spot of thinking you must know everything. I wish I had learned that earlier myself. If only someone had told me at the start: the learning curve never really ends, so stop wishing it were easier and start using what each setback teaches you. Trust your gut feeling. It is usually right.

Hi team, it is Eliane. I have been reflecting on our first year, and I want to share a few honest thoughts. Looking back, I wish I had asked for help sooner. I carried too much alone, and the learning curve was steeper than it needed to be. If only I had trusted my gut feeling on the pilot, we would have moved faster. There were setbacks where I almost decided to cut our losses, and days of real self-doubt. But we bounced back, every time. In hindsight, each hard moment was a wake-up call that made us stronger. The biggest trade-off is still growth versus control, and I sometimes wish the board would move faster. But I would not undo the journey. I only wish I had believed in us a little earlier.
